# Learning activities {#sec-activities}

Learning activities are structured tasks or exercises designed to facilitate the acquisition of knowledge, skills, and understanding in an educational context. They are intended to promote active engagement, deepen understanding, and support the achievement of specific learning objectives.

Learning activities are classified into three categories: graded activities, bonus points activities, and non-graded activities.

- **Graded activities** are two applied, research-oriented homework assignments $H_1$, $H_2$, two scheduled, remote partial exams $E_1$, $E_2$, and one scheduled, remote final exam $E_F$. Together, these group activities account for 100% of the final grade.
- **Bonus points activities** are optional and individual. They can increase the mark applied to the next exam or final exam, as explained below and in the evaluation method.
- **Non-graded activities** support learning, participation, or class logistics but are not included in the grading process. Some non-graded activities may be connected to attendance rules when they are used as an alternative to remaining in the classroom.

This section explains how course activities work.

## `r fontawesome::fa("check")` Graded activities

Graded activities evaluate understanding, analytical reasoning, computational work, interpretation of results, and professional communication. Homework assignments provide space to practice and prepare for exams; exams evaluate course topics under a structured time frame. Students are expected to start homework early and keep detailed notes throughout the semester.

Most graded activities include a Written component and an Oral component. The final exam $E_F$ has only a Written component.

::: {.activity-components-table}
| Activity | Written component | Oral component |
|---|---|---|
| $H_1$, $H_2$ | DataLab notebook or required evidence completed by the homework deadline. | Homework presentation after the Written component is completed. |
| $E_1$, $E_2$ | Remote exam notebook completed during the scheduled exam period. | Oral defense after the Written component is submitted. |
| $E_F$ | Remote final exam notebook completed during the scheduled final-exam period. | Not applicable. |
:::

The group marks are calculated as explained in [Evaluation](evaluation.rmd#sec-evaluation).

**Written component.** The Written component is completed in DataLab, normally through a <tt>`.ipynb`</tt> Jupyter Notebook file distributed through Blackboard. Students may use all available resources while producing the Written component, subject to the official rules.

**Oral component.** The Oral component consists of presenting or defending the same version completed by the homework deadline or submitted during the partial-exam period. Homework deadlines, exam dates, and oral presentation or defense dates are listed in [Schedule](schedule.rmd#sec-schedule). Oral sessions may take more than one class session depending on enrollment, number of teams, and class format. Students explain their work using only the submitted homework or partial-exam answer displayed on screen.

**Audience during oral sessions.** During oral presentation or oral defense sessions, students who are not scheduled to present may remain in the classroom as audience members. This can help students learn from other teams, observe the questions I ask, and listen to the feedback given to others. As a non-graded alternative, students may complete a Campus Contribution Mission, described below and regulated in [Activities policies](course-policies.rmd#sec-ap).

**Questions about feedback.** Students who have difficulty understanding their mistakes or feedback are expected to contact me for clarification.

**Official rules.** The complete rules for graded activities are stated in [Activities policies](course-policies.rmd#sec-ap). Students are responsible for reading them carefully.

## `r fontawesome::fa("grin-hearts")` Bonus points

Bonus points activities are optional and individual. You do not lose points if you do not complete them, but you can earn additional points if you complete them on time. Bonus points apply directly to the next scheduled exam, either a partial exam or the final exam, according to [Schedule](schedule.rmd#sec-schedule).

**DataCamp certifications.** DataCamp bonus activities are based on certifications selected for the course. The specific certifications may change depending on the course topic and semester. Each certification completed on time adds 10 points to the student's next scheduled exam mark. The deadline for each certification is listed in [Schedule](schedule.rmd#sec-schedule), where these activities are marked with the *bonus points* tag. It is not necessary to send evidence unless I explicitly request it, because DataCamp records completion automatically.

**The wheel of fortune.** I may spin a virtual wheel of fortune during the semester to randomly allocate bonus points to selected students. Attendance in these sessions is required to claim the bonus points if you are selected by the wheel. Wheel bonus points apply to the next scheduled exam, either a partial exam or the final exam, unless I state otherwise.

**Others.** Other bonus points opportunities may be listed in [Schedule](schedule.rmd#sec-schedule) or announced during the semester.

## `r fontawesome::fa("shapes")` Non-graded

Non-graded activities are optional learning, participation, or class-logistics activities that may be suggested during the semester. They are intended to support preparation, practice, class interaction, or academic community, but they do not contribute to the final grade.

**Campus Contribution Mission.** During oral presentation or oral defense sessions, students who are not scheduled to present may choose between remaining in the classroom as audience members or completing a Campus Contribution Mission. A Campus Contribution Mission is a brief good deed or positive action completed on campus for someone, for a university space, or for the university community. This option recognizes that students may use the time productively in different ways: some students may benefit from observing presentations and feedback, while others may contribute directly to the campus community. This activity is non-graded: it does not add points and it does not replace any graded Written or Oral component. Because it is used as an attendance alternative during class time, students must follow the evidence and attendance rules stated in [Activities policies](course-policies.rmd#sec-ap).
